name: Daimiel and Manzanares Circular
name_original: Manzanares desde Daimiel – circular por Villarrubia de los Ojos y Castillo de Pilas Bonas
description: This moderate 63.6-mile road cycling route from Daimiel explores rural landscapes and historical sites like Castillo de Pilas Bonas.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Daimiel are characterized by the remarkably flat terrain of the La Mancha region, offering expansive views of the Castilian landscape. The area features tranquil roads with minimal traffic, making it ideal for longer road cycling excursions. Many routes have low elevation gain, providing accessible options for various fitness levels. The defining natural feature is the Tablas de Daimiel National Park, a unique wetland ecosystem that offers serene backdrops for cycling. Best road cycling routes around Daimiel The most popular road cycling route is Daimiel and Manzanares Circular, a 63.6 miles (102.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 57 minutes to complete. This route offers a long-distance ride through the flat plains of the region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Carrión de Calatrava from Castillo de Calatrava La Vieja – circular through Malagón, el Embalse de Gasset and Fernán Caballero, a moderate 50.3 miles (81.0 km) path. This route features a low elevation gain of 667 feet (203 meters) across its length. Local road cyclists also love the Villarrubia de los Ojos from Parque Nacional de las Tablas de Daimiel – circular by Daimiel, a 55.3 miles (89.0 km) trail leading through the flat La Mancha landscape, often completed in about 3 hours 40 minutes. Road cycling around Daimiel is defined by flat plains, tranquil rural roads, and the unique wetland scenery of Tablas de Daimiel National Park. The network offers options for easy rides and more challenging moderate routes. The routes in Daimiel are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from a few reviews. More than 280 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Daimiel's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Daimiel?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes available around Daimiel, catering to various preferences. These include 4 easy routes, 18 moderate routes, and 2 more challenging options.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling in Daimiel?
A: Road cycling in Daimiel is characterized by the remarkably flat terrain of the La Mancha region. You'll find tranquil roads with minimal traffic and expansive views of the Castilian landscape. Most routes have low elevation gain, making them accessible for various fitness levels.

Q: What are the best times of year to go road cycling in Daimiel?
A: The ideal months for road cycling in Daimiel are typically September and October. During these months, the weather is generally pleasant, offering comfortable conditions for longer rides.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Daimiel?
A: Yes, Daimiel offers several easy road cycling routes, with 4 specifically categorized as easy. These routes are generally flat and shorter, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20128801" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Molino de Melocho – Fountain of Fortune loop from Daimiel&lt;/a&gt;, which is 24.5 km long. Within the Tablas de Daimiel National Park, there are also very flat, circular trails like the Isla del Pan itinerary (2.5 km) and the Laguna Permanente itinerary (1.5 km), which are perfect for a relaxed family ride.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes in Daimiel?
A: While the region is generally flat, there are 2 routes categorized as difficult for road cycling around Daimiel. These routes may feature longer distances or slightly more varied terrain compared to the many easy and moderate options available.

Q: What natural features can I see while road cycling around Daimiel?
A: The most prominent natural feature you'll encounter is the unique wetland ecosystem of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6285615" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tablas de Daimiel National Park&lt;/a&gt;. This area is renowned for its diverse birdlife and offers serene, picturesque backdrops. You can also find various &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6285603" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;observation points&lt;/a&gt; within the park for panoramic views.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, you can discover historical landmarks while cycling. The ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3694805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malvecinos Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, an old crossing over the Guadiana River, is located along some routes. Additionally, sections of the much longer 'La ruta de Don Quijote' pass through the Daimiel area, offering a cultural dimension to your ride. You might also pass by historical sites like Castillo de Pilas Bonas on some longer excursions.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes in Daimiel?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Daimiel are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1092583923/daimiel-and-manzanares-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Daimiel and Manzanares Circular&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1079504776/villarrubia-de-los-ojos-from-parque-nacional-de-las-tablas-de-daimiel-circular-by-daimiel" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villarrubia de los Ojos from Parque Nacional de las Tablas de Daimiel – circular by Daimiel&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Daimiel?
A: Daimiel, as a town, provides amenities and parking options for visitors. You can typically find parking in and around the town center or near the entrances to the Tablas de Daimiel National Park, making it convenient to start your cycling routes.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for cycling in certain areas, like Tablas de Daimiel National Park?
A: While general cycling on public roads does not require permits, specific regulations might apply to certain trails or areas within the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6285615" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tablas de Daimiel National Park&lt;/a&gt;. It's always advisable to check the park's official guidelines before entering, especially for protected zones or specific wooden walkways.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Daimiel?
A: The road cycling routes in Daimiel are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from numerous reviews. Cyclists often praise the flat plains, tranquil rural roads, and the unique wetland scenery of the Tablas de Daimiel National Park.

Q: Where can I find amenities like cafes or pubs along the routes?
A: Daimiel town itself offers a variety of amenities, including cafes and pubs, where you can refuel before or after your ride. Many routes start or pass through Daimiel, making it easy to access these services. For longer routes that venture further, it's recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, as services might be sparse in the more rural La Mancha steppe areas.
tips: The railway reached Daimiel on October 1, 1860 when the MZA company put into operation the Manzanares-Daimiel section of the line that sought to link Alcázar de San Juan with Ciudad Real.4 In 1941 the nationalization of the Iberian gauge network meant that the facilities will become dependent on the recently created RENFE. During the 1970s, the route was electrified, which would come into service on November 28, 1975.

In the 16th century, and due to population growth, the Daimiel authorities asked Emperor Carlos I for a license to build another parish church. The monarch agreed and the church was finished in 1587.

The church has a Latin cross floor plan, of the Doric order, brick vaults, stone arches and pottery tiled floors (today covered by a wooden platform).

The original altarpiece was golden, in the Churrigueresque style, and was destroyed during the Spanish Civil War.

Villarrubia de los Ojos is a charming place to visit!
Historic Center: Stroll through its streets and discover the traditional architecture and history of the town.
San Isidro Labrador Ethnographic Museum: Learn more about local culture and traditions.
Tablas de Daimiel National Park: Just 20 km away, this wetland is perfect for nature lovers.
Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Sierra: A place of great religious and cultural significance.

In Arenas de San Juan is the fortified Church of Nuestra Señora de las Angustias, which constitutes one of the most beautiful and original buildings of Romanesque-Mudejar art from the late 12th century and early 13th century, built and fortified by the knights of the Order of Santiago.
Its great artistic importance lies in the Romanesque mural paintings, which derive from the Italo-Byzantine movement that appeared in Spain at the beginning of the 13th century and constitute an almost unique example of Romanesque religious architecture that unites Mudejar forms in La Mancha. It was declared a Monument of Artistic Interest in 1976.
Over the Guadiana River, a bridge with twelve small ashlar arches.

The Manzanares Castle (Castillo de Pilas Bonas) is included in the Inventory of Architectural Heritage of Historical-Artistic Interest carried out by the Ministry of Culture in 1979. Although there are still many dark points regarding the origin of the Castle and the town of Manzanares, various historians agree that the Castle must have been built around the year 1239, after the demarcation of the territories of the orders of Santiago and Calatrava.
The reasons for its construction would probably be the proximity to the Azuer River, the existence of wells with abundant water or the crossing of roads and trails.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is predominantly paved, with approximately 96.4 kilometers on asphalt and an additional 5.81 kilometers on paved tracks. You'll encounter a very small cobbled section (about 144 meters) and minimal sections on unknown surfaces or trails. The ride primarily uses roads and national roads, with some urban street sections.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as 'moderate' for road cycling. While the elevation gain is relatively low, the significant distance of over 100 kilometers means it requires a good level of fitness and experience with longer rides. It might not be ideal for absolute beginners.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or towns will I pass through on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: You'll start near Daimiel Station and pass by the Church of San Pedro Apóstol (Daimiel). The route also takes you through the town of Villarrubia de los Ojos, past the Church of Our Lady of Sorrows (Arenas de San Juan), and includes the historical Castillo de Pilas Bonas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region around Manzanares generally experiences hot summers. Spring and autumn typically offer more pleasant temperatures for cycling, making them the best times to tackle this longer route. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where does the Manzanares from Daimiel circular route start, and what is the parking situation?
faqs.answer: The route is named 'Manzanares from Daimiel', indicating Daimiel as the starting point. A convenient starting point could be near Daimiel Station. Parking availability in Daimiel would typically include street parking or designated public parking areas within the town.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route that primarily uses public roads and national roads. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on a leash in public areas, the nature of road cycling makes it generally unsuitable for bringing dogs along, especially for such a long distance. Consider local parks or dedicated trails if you wish to cycle with your dog.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, this road cycling route primarily uses public roads and does not require any special permits or entrance fees. However, if you plan to visit specific attractions like the Castillo de Pilas Bonas, check their individual entry requirements.

faqs.question: Does this route pass through or near the Parque Nacional de las Tablas de Daimiel, and are there any specific regulations?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route starts from Daimiel, which is the gateway to the Parque Nacional de las Tablas de Daimiel. While the cycling route itself primarily sticks to roads outside the core protected areas, you will be in the vicinity. Cycling within the national park itself is often restricted to specific designated paths, and general park regulations regarding wildlife, waste, and noise should always be respected. Check the official park website for the most current rules on cycling within its boundaries.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect a mix of rural countryside and historical points of interest. The broader region is known for diverse landscapes, including granite rock formations, river paths, and varied forest and steppe environments. The route offers glimpses of local heritage, particularly with the 13th-century Castillo de Pilas Bonas.

faqs.question: Does this route follow any official or named trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route coincide with parts of other named paths. You will briefly follow portions of the Camino Natural del Guadiana and the Cañada Real Soriana Oriental.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or water along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns like Daimiel, Villarrubia de los Ojos, and Manzanares, you will find opportunities to stop for refreshments, food, and to refill water bottles. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient supplies, especially on longer rides, but these towns offer convenient breaks.

waypoints.name: Church of Our Lady of Sorrows (Arenas de San Juan)
